stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of thin grey-white vaginal discharge with a fishy odour, worse after intercourse. She has no vulval itch, no dysuria, no abnormal bleeding and no fever. She is in a monogamous relationship of 18 months using a copper IUD, last cervical screening 14 months ago was normal. Speculum: thin homogeneous off-white discharge coating the vaginal walls without inflammation. Vaginal pH at the lateral wall 5.2; KOH whiff test positive; wet mount shows clue cells, scant lactobacilli, no motile trichomonads, no pseudohyphae. She is not currently pregnant. She had a similar episode 5 months ago which was treated with a 7-day course of oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily; she completed the course, and symptoms resolved for 4 months before recurring.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: thin homogeneous grey-white discharge coating vaginal walls, no inflammation, no strawberry cervix.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.2. KOH whiff test positive. Wet mount: clue cells present, few lactobacilli, no motile trichomonads, no pseudohyphae.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the same 7-day oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily course
options.rationale: Repeating the same 7-day metronidazole alone is wrong because recurrent BV (>=3 episodes/year) needs re-induction PLUS suppressive maintenance therapy; standard courses alone have high recurrence rates due to residual biofilm. Would be correct only for a first or non-recurrent BV episode (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

options.text: Treat with oral metronidazole 400 mg bd for 7 days, then commence intravaginal metronidazole gel twice weekly for 4-6 months as suppressive therapy
options.rationale: Recurrent BV in Australia is managed with re-induction (oral or intravaginal metronidazole) followed by intravaginal metronidazole or clindamycin twice weekly as suppression for several months to reduce recurrence (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

options.text: Reassure that this is normal discharge and no treatment is needed
options.rationale: Reassurance is wrong because all four Amsel criteria are met (homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells); symptomatic BV warrants treatment. Would be correct only with normal discharge and no Amsel criteria met (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

options.text: Empirically treat both her and her male partner with metronidazole
options.rationale: Empirical partner treatment is wrong because routine treatment of male partners is not currently standard in Australian guidelines (though emerging evidence is evolving the picture); suppressive therapy for the index woman is the established next step. Would be correct only within research protocols or jurisdictions adopting recent partner-treatment data (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

options.text: Remove the copper IUD as it is the underlying cause of recurrent BV
options.rationale: IUD removal is wrong because the copper IUD has not been clearly established as a cause of recurrent BV and removal sacrifices effective contraception; suppressive vaginal therapy should be tried first. Would be correct only after suppressive therapy fails with strong temporal IUD association (Family Planning Australia).

explanation.summary: Bacterial vaginosis is diagnosed clinically by Amsel criteria (3 of 4: thin grey-white discharge, pH > 4.5, positive whiff with KOH, clue cells) or by Nugent score >= 7 on Gram stain. First-line treatment is oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days.
key_points: Amsel: 3 of 4 (discharge, pH > 4.5, whiff, clue cells)
Nugent score >= 7 confirms BV on Gram stain
Candida: itch, curdy, pH < 4.5, pseudohyphae
Trichomonas: frothy yellow-green, strawberry cervix, motile
Lactobacillus loss is the unifying pathophysiology of BV
explanation.pearls: A positive whiff test plus clue cells is virtually pathognomonic. BV is not classified as an STI but is associated with sexual activity and increases HIV/STI acquisition risk.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Bacterial vaginosis

subtopic_detail: Vaginal discharge - microscopy and Amsel recall

stem: A 21-year-old woman at 18 weeks of pregnancy presents with multiple soft non-keratinised fleshy lesions on the vulva and perianal area, first noticed 6 weeks ago and increasing in number. She is otherwise well with an uncomplicated pregnancy on midwifery shared care. She did not receive the HPV vaccine in school. On examination there are approximately 10 papillomatous lesions 2-5 mm distributed over the labia majora and perianal area; no internal vaginal, cervical or intra-anal lesions; cervical screening normal 4 months ago. She is keen to treat them before delivery and prefers home application.

data_table.Examination: Approximately 10 soft non-keratinised papillomatous lesions 2-5 mm on labia majora and perianal area. No cervical, vaginal, urethral or intra-anal lesions.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Cervical screening test 6 months ago: normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her anogenital warts?

options.text: Self-applied podophyllotoxin 0.5% solution twice daily for 3 days, then 4 days off, repeated up to 4 cycles
options.rationale: Podophyllotoxin is wrong because it is contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity (antimitotic activity); self-application is doubly inappropriate when she is pregnant. Would be correct only outside pregnancy (Australasian STI Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Self-applied imiquimod 5% cream three nights per week for up to 16 weeks
options.rationale: Imiquimod is wrong because imiquimod has limited pregnancy safety data and is not recommended in pregnancy; the immunomodulatory mechanism creates additional uncertainty. Would be correct only outside pregnancy (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Clinician-applied cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen weekly
options.rationale: Cryotherapy is safe in pregnancy and is the preferred first-line treatment for anogenital warts in pregnant women in Australian guidelines; trichloroacetic acid is an alternative. Self-applied options are contraindicated (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Watch and wait until after delivery as warts always regress postpartum
options.rationale: Watch and wait is wrong because anogenital warts may proliferate dramatically during pregnancy and obstruct the birth canal at term; antenatal treatment reduces burden at delivery rather than waiting for postpartum regression. Would be correct only for very small isolated lesions in late pregnancy (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

options.text: Plan elective caesarean section solely to prevent vertical HPV transmission
options.rationale: Elective caesarean for HPV is wrong because caesarean is not indicated solely for HPV transmission risk; vaginal delivery is acceptable unless warts physically obstruct the birth canal. Would be correct only with mechanical obstruction at term (Australasian STI Guidelines).
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts

explanation.summary: Patient-applied first-line options for external anogenital warts are imiquimod 5% cream three nights per week up to 16 weeks, or podophyllotoxin 0.5% solution. Clinician-applied options include cryotherapy, TCA and surgical removal. Counsel about HPV vaccination (Gardasil 9 covers HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58) regardless of past exposure.
key_points: Imiquimod 5%: 3 nights/week, wash off after 6-10 h, up to 16 weeks
Podophyllotoxin 0.5%: BD x 3 days then 4 days off, up to 4 cycles; avoid in pregnancy
Cryotherapy / TCA / excision are clinician-applied
HPV vaccine still recommended after warts diagnosis
Recurrence common; warts are not cancer-causing types (6/11)
explanation.pearls: HPV 6 and 11 cause 90% of anogenital warts and are not oncogenic. Cervical screening continues per national programme; warts diagnosis does not change screening intervals.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Anogenital warts
CDC STI Treatment Guidelines - Anogenital warts

stem: A 23-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right upper quadrant pain radiating to the right shoulder that has worsened over the past 36 hours, with a pleuritic component that is worse on deep inspiration and on lying flat. She also reports four days of lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia and intermenstrual bleeding. She has had two new male partners over the past three months and uses condoms inconsistently. She is not pregnant (serum beta-hCG < 5 IU/L today). On examination her temperature is 38.1 degrees C, pulse 104 regular, blood pressure 116/72, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 99% on room air. There is right upper quadrant tenderness without Murphy's sign, mild generalised lower abdominal tenderness without peritonism, and bilateral adnexal tenderness with cervical motion tenderness on bimanual examination. Speculum shows mucopurulent cervical discharge with contact bleeding. Liver function tests show mild transaminitis (ALT 78 U/L, AST 62 U/L) with normal bilirubin and ALP. Right upper quadrant ultrasound shows normal gallbladder and biliary tree with no stones, no wall thickening and no pericholecystic fluid. Pelvic ultrasound shows no tubo-ovarian abscess but trace pelvic free fluid. Endocervical NAAT is positive for Chlamydia trachomatis and negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 104 bpm, T 38.1 degC.
data_table.Examination: RUQ tender, Murphy's negative. Lower abdominal tenderness without peritonism. CMT, bilateral adnexal tenderness. Mucopurulent cervical discharge.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG < 5 IU/L. ALT 78, AST 62, bilirubin 14, ALP 92. RUQ US: normal GB, no stones. Pelvic US: no TOA, trace free fluid. NAAT: C. trachomatis positive.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis explaining the right upper quadrant pain?

options.text: Acute viral hepatitis A infection
options.rationale: Viral hepatitis A is wrong because acute HAV would produce more dramatic transaminitis (often >1000 U/L) and prodromal/systemic features but would not explain CMT, mucopurulent cervicitis, positive chlamydia NAAT or pleuritic RUQ pain; the constellation of PID plus RUQ pain points to perihepatitis. Would be correct only with isolated severe transaminitis and HAV serology positive (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute calculous cholecystitis with ascending cholangitis
options.rationale: Acute cholecystitis is wrong because ultrasound shows no stones, wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid, and Murphy's sign is negative; the combination essentially excludes cholecystitis. Would be correct with characteristic ultrasound findings and positive Murphy's sign (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Tubo-ovarian abscess with subdiaphragmatic spread
options.rationale: Tubo-ovarian abscess is wrong because pelvic ultrasound shows no TOA (trace free fluid is non-specific) and TOAs typically cause severe pelvic, not primarily RUQ, pain. Would be correct only with an imaged abscess and corresponding clinical picture (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum
options.rationale: Ruptured ectopic is wrong because serum beta-hCG <5 IU/L excludes pregnancy and therefore ectopic; this is a definitive exclusion. Would never be correct with a negative beta-hCG (RANZCOG).

options.text: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis perihepatitis (gonococcal or chlamydial)
options.rationale: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis is perihepatitis from haematogenous or transperitoneal spread of chlamydia (or gonorrhoea) complicating PID. RUQ pain with mild transaminitis, normal biliary imaging and concurrent PID with positive chlamydia NAAT fits the classical picture (ASHM; BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is perihepatitis complicating PID, classically due to Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae, producing RUQ pain with characteristic 'violin-string' adhesions between liver capsule and abdominal wall at laparoscopy. Mild transaminitis is common; biliary imaging is normal. Treatment is the same as for PID (ceftriaxone + doxycycline + metronidazole for 14 days).
key_points: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis = chlamydial/gonococcal perihepatitis
RUQ pain + PID + normal biliary tree
Violin-string adhesions on laparoscopy (classical)
Treat as PID (no different regimen)
Other PID complications: TOA, infertility, chronic pelvic pain, 6-10x ectopic risk
explanation.pearls: Suspect Fitz-Hugh-Curtis in any sexually active young woman with RUQ pain and pelvic findings, even when LFTs are only mildly deranged. It is a clinical and supportive imaging diagnosis - laparoscopy is rarely performed.

subtopic_detail: PID complication - right upper quadrant pain

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman with chronic HIV-1 infection is reviewed at 36+2 weeks gestation in the antenatal HIV clinic to plan delivery. She has been on tenofovir/emtricitabine/dolutegravir throughout this pregnancy, commenced at 9 weeks gestation, and her adherence has been excellent on pharmacy refill audit. Her booking CD4 count was 540 cells/microL with a baseline HIV viral load of 8400 copies/mL prior to recommencing therapy. Her most recent HIV viral load taken at 36+0 weeks is < 50 copies/mL (suppressed) and her last three viral loads in the third trimester (at 28, 32 and 36 weeks) have all been < 50 copies/mL. She had a normal vaginal birth with her previous pregnancy four years ago when she was already on suppressive cART. She has no obstetric contraindication to vaginal birth, the foetus is cephalic with appropriate interval growth on the 50th centile, there is no placenta praevia on the 32-week scan, and she has not had any complications this pregnancy. She is asking about the recommended mode of delivery, whether she requires intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, whether her baby will require post-exposure prophylaxis, and whether she can breastfeed.

data_table.Examination: Symphysio-fundal height 36 cm. Cephalic presentation. No oedema.
data_table.Investigations: HIV viral load at 36+0: < 50 copies/mL (suppressed). CD4 540 cells/microL. HBV/HCV negative. GBS swab pending. Growth scan 34 weeks: 50th centile, normal Dopplers.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for mode of delivery and neonatal prophylaxis?

options.text: Planned vaginal birth, no intrapartum zidovudine required, neonatal zidovudine monotherapy for 2-4 weeks postnatally
options.rationale: Vaginal birth plus 2-4 weeks zidovudine is wrong because Australian practice specifies 4 weeks of neonatal zidovudine for low-risk infants of suppressed mothers; the 2-4 week phrasing introduces imprecision and the question demands the precise duration. Would be correct only if the duration were stated as 4 weeks (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 39+0 weeks, intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal zidovudine for 6 weeks, no breastfeeding
options.rationale: Planned caesarean at 39+0 with IV zidovudine is wrong because viral load <50 copies/mL near term obviates the need for caesarean and intrapartum IV zidovudine; vaginal birth is preferred with suppression. Would be correct only with viral load >=50 copies/mL near term (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned caesarean section at 38+0 weeks, intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal triple-drug PEP for 4 weeks, breastfeeding permitted if exclusive
options.rationale: Planned caesarean plus triple-drug PEP plus breastfeeding is wrong on multiple counts: caesarean is reserved for unsuppressed viral load, triple-drug PEP is for higher-risk infants, and breastfeeding is not recommended in Australia regardless of suppression. Would never be correct in this scenario (ASHM).

options.text: Planned vaginal birth at term, no intrapartum intravenous zidovudine, neonatal zidovudine monotherapy for 4 weeks, no breastfeeding
options.rationale: When maternal HIV viral load is < 50 copies/mL near term, planned vaginal birth is recommended; intravenous intrapartum zidovudine is NOT required for suppressed viral load; the neonate receives oral zidovudine monotherapy for 4 weeks; breastfeeding is not recommended in high-income settings where safe formula is available (ASHM; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned vaginal birth with intrapartum intravenous zidovudine infusion regardless of viral load, neonatal nevirapine single dose only
options.rationale: Routine intrapartum IV zidovudine plus single-dose nevirapine is wrong because IV intrapartum zidovudine is reserved for unsuppressed/unknown viral load, and single-dose neonatal nevirapine is inadequate cover under Australian standards (oral zidovudine x 4 weeks is the regimen). Would be correct only with unsuppressed viral load and even then the neonatal regimen would differ (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Women on cART with viral load < 50 copies/mL near term can safely have a planned vaginal birth; intrapartum IV zidovudine is not required. Neonate receives 4 weeks of oral zidovudine monotherapy (low-risk). Formula feeding is recommended in Australia. Planned caesarean at 38-39 weeks is reserved for viral load >= 50-1000 (or unknown) near term, with IV zidovudine intrapartum and consideration of neonatal triple therapy.
key_points: VL < 50 at 36 weeks: vaginal birth, no IV zidovudine, neonatal AZT x 4 weeks
VL 50-1000: individualised; consider CS at 38-39 weeks
VL > 1000 or unknown: pre-labour CS at 38-39 weeks + IV zidovudine + neonatal triple PEP
Avoid invasive monitoring (FSE, FBS) if possible during labour
Formula feeding recommended in Australia (no breastfeeding)
explanation.pearls: Vertical transmission risk falls to < 1% (and approaches 0.1%) when maternal viral load is suppressed throughout pregnancy. The biggest modifiable factor is maternal adherence, not mode of delivery.

subtopic_detail: HIV in pregnancy - mode of delivery decision

stem: A 26-year-old woman attends the emergency department 28 hours after condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a male partner she has just learned is living with HIV-1. He told her his combination antiretroviral treatment was 'on and off' over the past six months because he had run out of his prescription, and she does not know his current viral load. She has no symptoms, no genital ulcerationosure, the partner does not inject drugs, and there was no concurrent intercourse with other partners. She is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative today), has no significant past medical history, is on no regular medications other than the etonogestrel implant, has no drug allergies, has normal renal function on baseline pathology (creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR > 90), is hepatitis B surface antibody positive (vaccinated as a teenager), HIV antibody/antigen negative on today's baseline 4th-generation test, hepatitis C antibody negative, and syphilis EIA negative. She is not breastfeeding and has no plans for pregnancy in the next month.

data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Baseline HIV Ag/Ab negative. HBsAb positive (vaccinated). HCV Ab negative. Syphilis EIA negative. Gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT sent. Creatinine 64 micromol/L, eGFR > 90.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis regimen and follow-up plan?

options.text: Single-dose tenofovir 300 mg given today, then simply repeat HIV testing again at the 6-week timepoint after exposure
options.rationale: Single-dose tenofovir is wrong because PEP requires a 28-day course of combination antiretrovirals; single-dose or monotherapy approaches do not prevent HIV acquisition. Would never be correct as PEP (ASHM PEP guidelines).

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg daily for 28 days, with no third agent required
options.rationale: Dual NRTI PEP is wrong because Australian guidance now recommends three-drug PEP (TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir or raltegravir) for high-risk exposures; dual NRTI-only PEP is outdated. Would be correct only under older guidelines, not current practice (ASHM).

options.text: Tenofovir alafenamide 25 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg plus rilpivirine 25 mg daily for 7 days only
options.rationale: TAF/FTC/rilpivirine 7 days is wrong because PEP duration is 28 days (not 7) and rilpivirine is not a standard third agent due to drug interactions and resistance considerations. Would never be a recommended PEP regimen (ASHM).

options.text: Tenofovir disoproxil/emtricitabine plus dolutegravir 50 mg daily for 28 days, with HIV testing at baseline, 6 weeks and 3 months
options.rationale: Australian PEP for a high-risk exposure (condomless receptive vaginal intercourse with a source person with HIV not on effective treatment, within 72 hours) is TDF/FTC plus dolutegravir for 28 days, with HIV testing at baseline, 6 weeks, and 3 months post-exposure (ASHM Australian PEP guidelines).

options.text: Defer PEP until the source partner's plasma viral load is known, then retest her at 4 weeks only
options.rationale: Defer PEP until source viral load is known is wrong because PEP efficacy falls rapidly with time and must start within 72 hours (ideally <24); waiting for source results risks missing the treatment window. Would be correct only if exposure were truly low risk and pending data could rapidly redirect care (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Non-occupational HIV PEP for a high-risk exposure (condomless receptive anal/vaginal sex with a source with HIV who is not virally suppressed, within 72 hours) is TDF 300 mg + FTC 200 mg + dolutegravir 50 mg daily for 28 days. Baseline tests: HIV Ag/Ab, syphilis, HBV, HCV, beta-hCG, renal function. Follow-up HIV tests at 6 weeks and 3 months (4th gen). Counsel on adherence, side effects, and condom use during follow-up.
key_points: Window: < 72 hours, ideally < 24 hours
Regimen: TDF/FTC + dolutegravir (or raltegravir) x 28 days
Baseline: HIV, syphilis, HBV, HCV, beta-hCG, U&E
Follow-up: HIV Ag/Ab at 6 weeks and 3 months
If source has confirmed undetectable VL, PEP may not be needed (U=U)
explanation.pearls: U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable): a source with confirmed sustained viral load < 200 copies/mL transmits HIV through sex effectively at zero risk - in those cases, PEP is not required.

stem: A 29-year-old cisgender woman attends a sexual health service requesting HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis. Her current male partner of three months has HIV-1 with a viral load that he reports has been detectable on most recent testing because of treatment interruption while changing jobs. They have condomless vaginal sex 2-3 times per week. She has no symptoms of acute HIV illness in the past 6 weeks (no fever, rash, lymphadenopathy or sore throat), is not pregnant (urine beta-hCG negative), is on the etonogestrel implant for contraception, has no history of renal disease, and is hepatitis B surface antibody positive (vaccinated). She has not injected drugs. Her last STI screen 8 months ago was negative. She has no allergies and no regular medications other than the implant. She is asking whether she is eligible for PrEP, what baseline tests are required, and what monitoring she will need on therapy.

data_table.Examination: Well. No mucosal lesions, no rash, no lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. HBsAg, anti-HBc and HBsAb pending today. HIV Ag/Ab pending today.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for PrEP initiation and monitoring?

options.text: Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg only after a 4-week negative HIV window-period repeat test
options.rationale: Deferring PrEP 4 weeks is wrong because a single recent negative HIV test plus absence of acute HIV symptoms is sufficient to start PrEP; deferring prolongs an unnecessary exposure window. Would be correct only if there are symptoms suggestive of acute seroconversion (ASHM PrEP guidelines).

options.text: Prescribe on-demand (2+1+1) tenofovir disoproxil plus emtricitabine, suitable for all genders in this clinical scenario today
options.rationale: On-demand (2+1+1) PrEP is wrong because on-demand dosing is only recommended for cisgender MSM with infrequent exposures; cisgender women require daily PrEP due to slower tissue drug levels in cervicovaginal tissue. Would be correct only for selected MSM, not for cisgender women (ASHM).

options.text: Prescribe daily tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg with baseline HIV/HBV/renal screen, then HIV/STI screening every 3 months
options.rationale: Daily oral TDF/FTC is the recommended PrEP regimen for cisgender women at substantial risk. Baseline workup includes HIV Ag/Ab, HBV serology, HCV antibody, syphilis serology, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT, renal function and beta-hCG. Quarterly monitoring includes HIV testing and STI screen; renal function 6-monthly (ASHM PrEP guidelines).

options.text: Defer PrEP and recommend condom use only, as PrEP is contraindicated when the partner has detectable viral load
options.rationale: Defer PrEP because partner is detectable is wrong because a partner with detectable viral load is exactly the indication for PrEP; PrEP is strongly indicated, not contraindicated. Would never be correct (ASHM).

options.text: Prescribe daily oral dolutegravir 50 mg as PrEP monotherapy in this scenario at first visit
options.rationale: Dolutegravir monotherapy is wrong because dolutegravir is not a PrEP agent; PrEP uses TDF/FTC (daily or, for selected MSM, on-demand). Would never be correct (ASHM).

explanation.summary: Cisgender women eligible for PrEP receive daily oral TDF 300 mg + FTC 200 mg. Baseline workup: HIV Ag/Ab, HBV serology, HCV antibody, syphilis EIA, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT (multi-site), renal function, beta-hCG. Quarterly: HIV test, STI screen, adherence and side-effect review. Renal function 6-monthly. On-demand (2+1+1) dosing only for cisgender MSM with predictable infrequent exposures. PrEP can be continued in pregnancy if substantial risk persists.
key_points: Daily TDF/FTC for cisgender women (no on-demand)
MSM may use daily or on-demand (2+1+1)
Baseline: HIV, HBV, HCV, syphilis, GC/CT, renal, beta-hCG
Monitoring: HIV + STI every 3 months; renal every 6 months
HBV must be screened pre-PrEP (rebound on cessation)
explanation.pearls: Exclude acute HIV before initiation: ask about fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, sore throat in the past 6 weeks. Acute HIV may have a still-negative Ag/Ab and starting PrEP could select for resistance.

subtopic_detail: HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis - eligibility and monitoring

stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department at 0300 hours, brought by police, reporting that she was sexually assaulted approximately 14 hours ago at a private residence by an unknown male. The reported assault involved vaginal penetration without a condom, and she believes ejaculation occurred. She has showered once since the assault but has not changed her underwear, brushed her teeth, eaten or passed urine since the event. She has no significant medical history, is on no regular medications, has no drug allergies, has had a normal menstrual period that finished 8 days ago, and is sexually active with a regular partner from whom she uses no contraception. She is alert, oriented, distressed but cooperative, has minor bruising on her right upper arm and a small abrasion on her left knee, and has not previously had a forensic examination. There is no immediate life-threatening injury, no head injury, no loss of consciousnesspromise, and she has not consumed alcohol or other substances in the past 24 hours. A specially trained forensic medical examiner is available on call at the local sexual assault service, with chain-of-custody documentation procedures in place, and a social worker can be paged. She is asking what will happen next, what medications she can be offered tonight to reduce pregnancy and infection risk, and whether the examination is mandatory. She has not yet decided whether to formally report to police and is uncertain about disclosing to her family.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Alert, distressed, cooperative. Bruise 3 cm on right upper arm. No external genital injury visible without colposcopic examination.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. Baseline HIV Ag/Ab, HBsAg/Ab, HCV Ab, syphilis EIA, gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT and trichomonas NAAT sent. Pregnancy and STI follow-up planned per local sexual assault service protocol.

lead_in: Which set of medical and forensic actions is the most appropriate tonight?

options.text: Defer forensic examination until daylight to reduce patient distress, commence empirical STI prophylaxis and offer emergency contraception
options.rationale: Defer forensic examination until daylight is wrong because forensic evidence degrades within the <72-hour window; delaying loses DNA, secretions and trace evidence regardless of patient distress (a trained sexual assault examiner is best placed to balance comfort with timeliness). Would never be correct (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).

options.text: Proceed only with forensic examination tonight; defer STI prophylaxis and emergency contraception until after kit processed
options.rationale: Forensic-only first is wrong because medical priorities (EC within 120 hours, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP within 72 hours, HBV cover, safety planning) cannot be deferred to await kit processing; they run in parallel with forensic care. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Forensic examination can only proceed if the patient agrees to formally report the assault to police today
options.rationale: Linking forensic exam to police reporting is wrong because forensic examination should be offered regardless of reporting intent, with anonymous kit storage available to preserve future options; mandating reporting acts as a barrier to disclosure. Would never be correct under Australian best practice (RANZCOG; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance).

options.text: Forensic examination is contraindicated because the patient has showered; reassure and discharge with general practice follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Showered as contraindication is wrong because showering reduces but does not eliminate forensic yield; trace DNA and other evidence can still be recovered, and discharging without addressing EC, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP, HBV cover and safety is unsafe. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Offer immediate forensic examination with chain-of-custody, emergency contraception, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP if eligible, hepatitis B vaccination and safety planning
options.rationale: Acute sexual assault management within 72 hours requires concurrent attention to medical priorities (EC within 120 hours, STI prophylaxis, HIV PEP assessment within 72 hours, HBV protection, mental health support and safety planning) and forensic priorities (kit collection with chain of custody by a trained examiner), regardless of whether the patient has decided to report (RANZCOG; ASHM; Australasian Sexual Health Alliance). Full care includes: forensic examination by trained examiner with documented chain of custody; LNG or UPA or copper IUD for emergency contraception; ceftriaxone IM plus azithromycin or doxycycline plus metronidazole for empirical STI prophylaxis; HIV PEP if eligible; hepatitis B vaccination (and HBIG if indicated); safety planning, counselling and follow-up.
source_refs: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Sexual assault

explanation.summary: Acute presentation within 72 hours of sexual assault requires concurrent medical and forensic priorities: forensic examination with chain of custody by a trained examiner regardless of reporting intent; emergency contraception (levonorgestrel up to 96 hours, ulipristal up to 120 hours, or copper IUD up to 5 days); STI baseline tests and prophylaxis (ceftriaxone 500 mg IM, azithromycin 1 g or doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days, metronidazole 2 g single dose); HIV PEP eligibility assessment for source with HIV or unknown source with high-risk features; hepatitis B vaccination and HBIG where indicated; safety planning; mental health support and follow-up at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, and 3 months.
key_points: Forensic kit window: 72 hours (some up to 7 days for specific samples)
EC: levonorgestrel < 96 h, ulipristal < 120 h, Cu-IUD < 5 days
STI prophylaxis: ceftriaxone + azithromycin/doxycycline + metronidazole
HIV PEP: assess within 72 h; high-risk sources warrant PEP
HBV: vaccinate if unvaccinated; HBIG if source HBsAg-positive
Forensic exam offered regardless of reporting intent
explanation.pearls: Documentation matters - use trained examiners (FAMU/SARC), follow chain-of-custody, photograph injuries, store kit per jurisdiction protocol. Mental-health and safety planning are not afterthoughts; ongoing risk of further violence is high in the first weeks.

sources.label: Australasian STI Management Guidelines - Sexual assault

subtopic_detail: Acute sexual assault - forensic and medical priorities

stem: A 32-year-old woman who is breastfeeding her 4-month-old infant (exclusive breastfeeding, no formula) presents with 3 weeks of frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge, vulval irritation and dyspareunia. Strawberry cervix on speculum, vaginal pH 5.6, KOH whiff positive, saline wet mount shows motile flagellated protozoa. Gonorrhoea and chlamydia NAAT have been sent. She has had two new male partners in the past 4 months. She has no allergies and no liver disease. She wishes to continue breastfeeding without interruption if possible.

data_table.Examination: Strawberry cervix with punctate haemorrhages. Copious frothy yellow-green vaginal discharge. No cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 5.6. Saline wet mount: motile flagellated protozoa. Trichomonas NAAT pending. Gonorrhoea/chlamydia NAAT sent. Urine beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Single 2 g oral metronidazole dose to her and her recent partners; pause breastfeeding for 12-24 hours and discard expressed milk
options.rationale: Single-dose metronidazole 2 g is first-line for trichomoniasis in adults, including breastfeeding women, with brief interruption of breastfeeding (12-24 hours) and discarding milk during peak levels to minimise infant exposure. Partner treatment is essential to prevent reinfection (Australasian STI Guidelines; eTG).

options.text: Intravaginal metronidazole 0.75% gel nightly for 5 nights; no partner treatment
options.rationale: Intravaginal metronidazole gel is wrong because topical metronidazole achieves <50% cure for trichomoniasis (parasites in urethra, glands, etc. not reached); systemic therapy is required and partners must be treated to prevent ping-pong reinfection. Would never be correct for trichomonas (Australasian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Tinidazole 2 g orally as a single dose with continued breastfeeding throughout
options.rationale: Tinidazole with continued breastfeeding is wrong because tinidazole has a longer half-life requiring an approximately 3-day breastfeeding pause; metronidazole 2 g single dose with a 12-24h pause is preferred in breastfeeding. Would be correct only outside breastfeeding (Australasian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Permanently cease breastfeeding so that metronidazole can be given safely
options.rationale: Permanently cease breastfeeding is wrong because a brief 12-24h pause is sufficient to clear peak metronidazole levels; permanent cessation is unnecessary and harmful. Would never be correct (Australasian STI Guidelines).

options.text: Doxycycline 100 mg bd for 7 days to her only
options.rationale: Doxycycline to her only is wrong because doxycycline has no activity against Trichomonas vaginalis and is contraindicated in breastfeeding due to enamel staining; treating her alone also leaves partners to reinfect. Would never be correct for trichomoniasis (eTG; ASHM).

explanation.summary: Trichomoniasis in non-pregnant adults is treated with oral metronidazole 2 g single dose, or 400 mg BD for 7 days (alternative for those at higher risk of treatment failure such as HIV). In pregnancy, treatment is still recommended (oral metronidazole 400 mg BD for 5-7 days). Always treat sexual partners empirically. Test of cure not routine but re-test for reinfection at 3 months.
key_points: First-line: metronidazole 2 g PO stat OR 400 mg BD x 7 d
Strawberry cervix (colpitis macularis) in 2-5% but pathognomonic when present
Motile flagellated protozoa on wet mount confirms; NAAT most sensitive
Partner notification and empirical treatment essential
Pregnancy: treat with oral metronidazole; avoid topical preparations
explanation.pearls: Avoid alcohol during and for 24 hours after metronidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). HIV co-infection increases treatment failure risk; consider 7-day BD regimen in that group.
